name: La Tijera from Candelada — circular in the Parque Regional de la Sierra de Gredos
name_original: R1: La Tijera desde Candelada — circular en el Parque Regional de la Sierra de Gredos
region_intro: Hiking around Candeleda offers diverse terrain at the foot of the Sierra de Gredos mountains and within the Tiétar Valley. The region features rugged landscapes, glacial valleys, and lush riverside forests. Numerous natural pools and gorges, such as the Garganta de Santa María, are formed by crystal-clear waterways. The area is characterized by a rich biodiversity, including forests of chestnut, ash, and alder trees. Best hiking trails around Candeleda The most popular hiking route is La Tijera from Candelada — circular in the Parque Regional de la Sierra de Gredos, a 4.9 miles (7.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 28 minutes to complete. This moderate circular route explores the regional park. Another top favourite among local hikers is Almanzor via the Tío Domingo path from the Hornillo Platform — round trip in the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park, a difficult 10.4 miles (16.8 km) path. This challenging route offers significant elevation gain towards Pico Almanzor. Local hikers also love the R2: La Raya - Arroyo de Castañarejo from Candeleda — circular in the Parque Regional de la Sierra de Gredos, a 9.6 miles (15.5 km) trail leading through the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park, often completed in about 5 hours 4 minutes. Hiking around Candeleda is defined by the imposing Sierra de Gredos mountains, the lush Tiétar Valley, and numerous natural gorges. The network offers options for easy strolls, moderate paths, and demanding mountain ascents. The routes in Candeleda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 70 reviews. More than 400 hikers have used komoot to explore Candeleda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Candeleda?
A: Candeleda offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 35 routes recorded on komoot. These range from easy strolls to challenging mountain ascents, catering to all fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain and natural features can I expect on Candeleda's hiking trails?
A: The trails around Candeleda traverse diverse landscapes, from the rugged, glacial valleys of the Sierra de Gredos mountains to the lush, riverside forests of the Tiétar Valley. You'll encounter crystal-clear natural pools and gorges, such as the Garganta de Santa María, and forests rich in chestnut, ash, and alder trees. The region is also known for its impressive rock formations and offers opportunities to spot wildlife like ibex.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Candeleda?
A: Yes, Candeleda has several easy and moderate trails suitable for families. There are 7 easy routes and 18 moderate routes available, offering accessible options to enjoy the natural beauty of the area without overly strenuous climbs. These paths often lead through varied landscapes and along waterways, providing engaging experiences for all ages.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Candeleda?
A: Yes, many routes in Candeleda are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the moderate route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1380982656/la-tijera-from-candelada-circular-in-the-parque-regional-de-la-sierra-de-gredos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Tijera from Candelada — circular in the Parque Regional de la Sierra de Gredos&lt;/a&gt;, which is 4.9 miles (7.9 km) long and explores the regional park.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest to see while hiking in Candeleda?
A: While hiking around Candeleda, you can discover several interesting landmarks. The historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6316610" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge of Madrigal de la Vera&lt;/a&gt; is a significant sight. You might also encounter mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/540292" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Candeleda&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views. The area also features the ancient Castro de El Raso, a Betón Celtic settlement, which provides historical context to your outdoor adventures.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Candeleda?
A: Candeleda is an excellent hiking destination year-round, but the best time often depends on your preferred activity. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for longer treks, with vibrant flora or colorful foliage. Summer is ideal for enjoying the natural pools and gorges, while winter can provide challenging, snow-covered mountain experiences in the higher Sierra de Gredos, though lower trails remain accessible.

Q: Are there challenging hikes for experienced trekkers in Candeleda?
A: Absolutely. For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Candeleda offers several difficult routes with significant elevation gain. A prime example is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1380975672/almanzor-via-the-tio-domingo-path-from-the-hornillo-platform-round-trip-in-the-sierra-de-gredos-regional-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almanzor via the Tío Domingo path from the Hornillo Platform&lt;/a&gt;, a 10.4-mile (16.8 km) round trip in the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park that leads towards Pico Almanzor, the highest peak in the range.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Candeleda?
A: The trails in Candeleda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 70 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse terrain, from the dramatic mountain scenery to the refreshing natural pools, and the well-developed network of trails that cater to various fitness levels.

Q: Are there any trails that lead to natural swimming spots or gorges?
A: Yes, Candeleda is renowned for its abundance of crystal-clear natural pools and gorges, formed by rivers like the Garganta de Santa María. Popular spots for cooling off include Charco Carreras and El Palomas. Many trails follow these waterways, offering opportunities for refreshing dips during warmer months.

Q: Can I access hiking trails in Candeleda using public transport?
A: While Candeleda itself is accessible by public transport, direct access to all trailheads might require local taxis or a short walk from the town center. Some long-distance paths, like the GR 180 "Valle del Tiétar," have stages that pass through Candeleda, making parts of them accessible from the town.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Candeleda?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many hiking trails in the Candeleda area, especially in the natural environment. However, it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, particularly in protected areas like the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park, to protect local wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Please be mindful of local regulations and signage.

Q: Is there a trail that combines natural beauty with historical interest?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2359874886/raso-and-the-castro-celta-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Raso and the Castro Celta Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 7.9-mile (12.7 km) route that offers a blend of natural beauty and historical significance. This trail allows you to explore the impressive archaeological site of Castro de El Raso, a Betón Celtic settlement, while enjoying the surrounding landscapes.
tips:
In this roundabout in Candeleda we can find this monument, which honors the mountain goat, also called Hispanic goat, without a doubt the queen of this mountain range.
The work is from 1980.
This sign welcomes us to the Sierra de Gredos regional park. In it, we can also observe some of the things that are and are not allowed to be done.

The largest oak forest in the town of Candelada. There is a signposted route (R1: La Tijera) that allows you to enter this beautiful place. There is no fountain during the tour, so you have to stock up on water in Candelada. The route is not difficult at all, it can be done perfectly with the whole family, all year round, although I recommend doing it in spring or autumn.
